summary: I was involved with the Washington State Rainbow Coalition from its early days around 1984, especially during the Jesse Jackson campaigns. We met at the Central Area Motivation Project, and the coalition served as a supportive structure for various communities including Black, Latino, Asian American, and LGBTQ groups, although it was not a mass organization. My role was more as a liaison to the LGBTQ community within the coalition, and I worked alongside leaders like Larry Gossett and folks from El Centro de la Raza. The coalition helped cross-fertilize support among communities even as individual community organizing grew stronger.

I moved to Washington state in 1975 after being fired from Cornell University. I came here to join a new program at the University of Washington that focused on the social management of technology. Since moving here, I became deeply involved in local politics and activism, including the Rainbow Coalition and the ACLU. Washington state became the center of much of my community and political work, especially in Seattle and King County.

Before moving to Washington, I was on the faculty at Cornell University for five years, where I taught and published work. I was fired from Cornell, which prompted my move to Seattle in 1975. My experience there shaped my academic and professional background, especially since I had both law and regional planning degrees. Leaving Cornell was a turning point that led me to new opportunities at the University of Washington.

I joined the University of Washington in 1975 with tenure, which was crucial given the controversial nature of my work evaluating technologies from social, political, and legal perspectives. I was part of a new program on social management of technology, housed in the College of Engineering. The university became my professional home and base for my activism, including my involvement with the Rainbow Coalition and the ACLU. The tenure protected me from pressures by donors who were wary of critical analyses of their technologies.

entity: College of Engineering, University of Washington (Organization)
summary: My appointment at the University of Washington was through the College of Engineering, where I worked on evaluating technologies from social and legal perspectives. This was somewhat unusual because many donors to the college represented firms that did not want critical scrutiny of their technologies. Having tenure through the College of Engineering gave me protection to pursue my controversial work and community activism without fear of losing my position.

My involvement in politics started early in life in New York City, where my mother and I worked on reform efforts within the Democratic Party during the 1950s and 1960s. Politics was part of my family culture, with discussions about current affairs going back generations. This foundation influenced my later political activism, including my work with the Rainbow Coalition and support for Jesse Jackson's presidential campaigns in the 1980s.

I grew up in New York City and was politically active there from a young age, especially with my mother in Democratic Party reform efforts during the 1950s and 1960s. My family had a tradition of discussing political and social issues, which shaped my lifelong interest in activism. Before moving to Washington in 1975, I was deeply connected to the political and social movements in New York.

I was involved with Jesse Jackson's presidential campaigns, particularly his second run in 1988 when he spoke at the Seattle Center. I introduced him at that event and was active in the Washington State Rainbow Coalition, which grew out of support for his campaigns starting around 1984. Jackson's campaigns were a focal point for coalition-building among diverse communities in Washington, and I worked alongside local organizers to support his efforts.

Jesse Jackson spoke at the Seattle Center during his 1988 presidential campaign, at the mural amphitheater. I was asked by local organizers to introduce him at that event, which was a significant moment in the Rainbow Coalition's activities. The Seattle Center served as a public venue for political events and community gatherings related to progressive causes during that period.

The Central Area Motivation Project, or CAMP, was where the early meetings of the Washington State Rainbow Coalition took place, specifically on 18th Avenue. It was a hub for community organizing and activism in Seattle's Central District. I attended these meetings and worked alongside other activists who were involved in the coalition's formation and activities.

In 1979, I traveled illegally to Cuba through arrangements made by the Center for Cuban Studies in New York City. We left from Montreal to avoid passport stamps that would have caused problems, and I received a Cuban entry stamp on a separate piece of paper. This trip was part of my broader political and social activism, and it connected me with progressive movements and perspectives that influenced my later work in Washington.

The Center for Cuban Studies in New York City organized the trip to Cuba that I took in 1979. They arranged for us to leave from Montreal to avoid passport complications, which allowed me to visit Cuba despite U.S. travel restrictions. This experience was part of my early activism and broadened my understanding of international social justice issues.

I became very active in the American Civil Liberties Union in Washington state, serving on the board of the state affiliate and later on the National Board for twenty-five years. Much of my activism, especially around civil liberties and LGBTQ rights, was channeled through the ACLU. The organization was deeply involved in battles to protect civil rights legislation in Seattle, including protections for gay people in housing and employment.

During the late 1980s AIDS crisis, I became involved in the formation of ACT UP, the AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power. This was after my earlier work with the Rainbow Coalition and the ACLU. ACT UP was a critical part of my activism focused on health and civil rights issues affecting the LGBTQ community during that difficult period.

Since moving to Seattle in 1975, it has been the center of my academic and activist life. I worked at the University of Washington and became involved in local politics and community organizations like the Rainbow Coalition and the ACLU. Seattle's Central District and places like the Seattle Center were key locations for political events and organizing efforts that I participated in over the years.

I knew Larry Gossett through the Rainbow Coalition and worked with him on various community and political activities. In 1993, when he decided to run for the King County Council, he asked me to give the keynote speech at his kickoff event. Larry's election to the council marked an important moment in local progressive politics, and I was proud to support him.

Larry Gossett was elected to the King County Council around 1993, and I was involved in supporting his campaign by giving the keynote speech at his kickoff event. The council became a platform for progressive political leadership in the region, and my connection to it was through my work with Larry and the Rainbow Coalition. This was part of the broader political activism I engaged in during the early 1990s in Washington state.

I worked with people from El Centro de la Raza as part of my involvement in the Rainbow Coalition and community organizing in Seattle. El Centro was a key organization representing the Latino community and was part of the coalition's diverse membership. My interactions with them helped strengthen cross-community support and political activism in the region.

I knew Bob Santos as one of the active leaders in the Rainbow Coalition and community activism in Seattle. He was prominent in representing the Asian American community and worked alongside other leaders in the coalition. My work with Bob and others helped build a multi-ethnic coalition focused on progressive political and social issues in Washington state.

Although Bernie Whitebear was a prominent Native American leader in Seattle during the time of the Rainbow Coalition, I did not know him personally. He was well known for his activism in the Native American community, which was one of the many communities involved in progressive organizing in the region. My connections were more with other ethnic communities and progressive white activists.

Martin Luther King Jr. Way in Seattle's Central District was a significant location for African American history and activism. It housed institutions like the African American History Museum and was near places like El Centro de la Raza. This street and neighborhood were part of the broader community context in which the Rainbow Coalition and other progressive organizations operated.

The Central District in Seattle was a hub for much of the community organizing and activism I was involved in, including the early meetings of the Rainbow Coalition at CAMP on 18th Avenue. It was a diverse neighborhood with strong African American, Latino, Asian American, and Native American communities. The Central District's history of activism and cultural institutions shaped the coalition's work and the broader progressive movement in Seattle.
